Player 4: As a Servant with no prior quest data, I have no concrete role reads yet. On round 1, approving a 2-player team is usually best to gather information rather than risk early rejection chains. The proposed team [0,2] is not obviously suspicious, and my vote reveals little beyond a standard good-side preference for early information.
Player 3: No concrete role information yet from quest outcomes or voting patterns. As a Good Servant on round 1 quest 1, a 2-person team of [0,2] is a standard opening proposal and rejecting without evidence gives little benefit while risking unnecessary early rejection. Approving reveals limited information but helps generate quest data; if it fails, suspicion narrows to 0 or 2.
Player 1: I know player 0 is my evil teammate, while players 2/3/4 are good. In team voting on quest 1, an evil team containing 0 and a good player is strong for evil because it can either fail immediately or create ambiguity depending on later play. As Assassin, I should support evil-access teams early, especially one with my teammate on it. Approving reveals little because good players may also approve an opening team.
Player 0: I know Player 1 is my evil teammate; Player 2 is currently unknown good/evil but not on my visible evil list, so likely good. On round 1 quest 1, a team of [0,2] includes me, giving evil direct control to fail if needed, though early failed first quests can expose me. As evil, approving a team with myself on it is generally strong because it either lets me fail for tempo or pass to build cover. My vote approving also looks natural since I proposed it and rejecting my own team would be suspicious. The information revealed is minimal and approval helps evil preserve flexibility.

Player 4: Quest 1 succeeded with team [0,2], so 0 and 2 are mildly good-leaning but not cleared. Proposed team [1,2,3] includes 2 from the successful mission and adds two unknowns, which is a reasonable exploratory good-team for quest 2. As a Servant, I want to approve plausible teams that include at least one success-proven player rather than reject without evidence. My vote reveals willingness to test new information while keeping some reliability via player 2.
Player 3: Player 2 was on the successful first quest, so they are somewhat more trusted. Player 1 is untested, and I know I am Good, so this proposed team has at least one confirmed Good (me) plus one somewhat trusted player. On team vote as Good, approving a reasonable low-risk team early is optimal, especially since excluding Player 0 after a success may still generate information. My approve vote supports a team with me and preserves tempo; rejecting would give little extra info here.
Player 0: I am Evil Minion with teammate 1. Proposed team [1,2,3] contains exactly one evil (1), which is good for us because it gives evil the option to fail quest 2 and take parity at 1-1. Player 1 as leader selecting self plus 2 and 3 is also reasonably non-suspicious after quest 1 succeeded with [0,2], where I passed to avoid exposure. Approving helps evil; rejecting only delays and may risk a worse team for us. My vote itself reveals little since many players would approve an apparently reasonable 3-person team.
Player 1: I know player 0 is my evil teammate; players 2 and 3 are currently unconfirmed. Quest 1 succeeded on team [0,2], which is consistent with 0 choosing to pass to avoid early suspicion, so 2 remains uncertain rather than cleared. We are in team voting on Quest 2. As evil, I generally want flexibility: approving a team with me on it gives me the option to fail, and a team of three containing me but not 0 avoids linking both evils together. Approving also looks natural since I proposed this team myself; rejecting my own team would be highly suspicious and reveal anti-good intent. My vote reveals little beyond consistency with my proposal, which helps evil maintain cover.

experiences.intentions:
Player 4: Two successful quests with teams [0,2] and [1,2,3] imply players 2 and 3 are currently fairly trustworthy; at minimum, 2 was on both successes and 3 was on a success. As a Good Servant in team voting, I should generally approve teams that are consistent with known-success configurations. Approving [2,3] supports a strong-looking team and gives useful info if it somehow fails, without needlessly risking rejection cycles.
Player 3: I am Good Servant. Two quests have succeeded with teams [0,2] and [1,2,3], both with 0 fails. That strongly suggests 2 and likely 3 are good; as 3, I know I am good, so team [2,3] contains two players with strong good evidence. On quest 3 with a 2-person team in 5-player Avalon, approving a compact team of proven-success players is optimal for Good. Approving reveals little harmful information and helps lock in a likely winning team.
Player 0: I know Player 1 is my evil teammate; players 2 and 3 are currently being trusted because both prior quests succeeded with 0 fails. As evil, with Good already up 2-0, I want to create doubt and avoid clean all-good teams passing. The current phase is team voting on [2,3]; approving risks a likely third success if both are good. Rejecting is optimal to push for a different team and preserve room for evil influence. My reject signals some suspicion of this team, which can help sow uncertainty without directly outing alignment.
Player 2: I am Merlin and know 0 and 1 are Evil, so 3 and 4 are the remaining non-seen players with at least one definitely Good among them; since only two Evil exist in 5-player Avalon and I already see both, both 3 and 4 must be Good. The proposed team [2,3] excludes known Evil players and keeps a clean 2-person team after two successful quests. As Good/Merlin, optimal play is to approve strong teams that can secure the third success. Approving reveals little beyond supporting a reasonable clean team, which helps Good more than it helps Evil.
Player 1: I know player 0 is my evil teammate. Quests 1 and 2 both succeeded with teams [0,2] and [1,2,3], meaning either evil chose to pass on quest 1 or the non-0 members are good; player 2 has appeared on all successful teams and looks trusted. Current phase is team voting on quest 3, where a 2-player team [2,3] is invalid for standard Avalon quest 3 and, more importantly, excludes both evil players. As evil, I want to prevent another likely success and avoid helping good lock in trusted all-good teams. Rejecting reveals little because rejecting a team without me or 0 is reasonable from many perspectives and helps evil stall for better sabotage opportunities.
